food_subgroup: Paneer dishes / Indian cottage cheese preparations

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for paneer saagwala; closest_parent is the general cottage cheese food product class. A more specific Indian paneer curry term is absent from FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Cheese dishes) is the closest defensible parent; no specific paneer saagwala or saag paneer code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for paneer saagwala or saag paneer as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of paneer saagwala is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of paneer cubes in spinach-cream gravy, usually accompanied by bread or rice ordered separately.

nutrient_source.title: Palak Paneer (Spinach with Cottage Cheese) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports palak paneer as a composite dish. Paneer saagwala is the same dish under a regional name variant (saag = leafy greens, often spinach). Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN palak paneer data. Cream content in restaurant versions may elevate fat slightly; values reflect a standard home-style preparation as reported by ICMR-NIN, which is the closest approved primary source for this Indian dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for paneer saagwala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 based on the analogous food profile: paneer (fresh cheese, GI ~27 per University of Sydney GI Database) combined with spinach (non-starchy leafy green, negligible glycemic contribution) and a small amount of cream. The composite dish is low-carbohydrate and high-protein/fat, consistent with a low GI. GL per serving calculated as (5.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving / 100) × 35 / 100 = 4.7 g. GI category: low (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g); restaurant versions may contain significantly more sodium due to added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 250 g restaurant serving may contribute approximately 775 mg sodium, representing roughly one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low net carbohydrate content (~5.4 g per 100 g); high protein and fat slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer saagwala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (150–200 g), as its low GI and high protein content support stable postprandial blood glucose; pairing with low-GI bread (e.g. roti) rather than white rice further reduces glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~5.1 g per 100 g) from paneer and cream; however, spinach provides beneficial folate, potassium, and antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake to 150 g per serving and choose preparations using minimal cream or ghee, as the saturated fat from dairy components can contribute to LDL cholesterol elevation when consumed in large quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer saagwala (also widely known as saag paneer or palak paneer) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry in which cubes of fresh Indian cottage cheese (paneer) are simmered in a smooth, spiced purée of leafy greens — most commonly spinach (palak) — enriched with cream, onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, and garam masala. The dish has a rich, velvety green gravy with a mildly earthy, slightly bitter spinach flavour balanced by the mild creaminess of the paneer and the warmth of the spices. Paneer cubes are typically pan-fried or added directly to the gravy, absorbing the spiced sauce while retaining a soft, slightly chewy texture. Nutritionally, paneer saagwala is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 138 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 9.8 g fat, and only 5.4 g carbohydrate, making it one of the more nutritionally balanced Indian restaurant dishes. The spinach component contributes meaningful amounts of iron (2.4 mg/100 g), vitamin A (145 mcg RAE/100 g), folate (55 mcg DFE/100 g), and vitamin C (12 mg/100 g). The paneer and cream contribute calcium (210 mg/100 g) and saturated fat. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. Clinically, the dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low GI and high protein content. Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, particularly in restaurant preparations where salt levels may be higher. In Singapore, paneer saagwala is served at Indian restaurants and is popular among the South Asian diaspora and broader population seeking vegetarian Indian cuisine. It is typically served with naan, roti, or basmati rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in saag paneer?
Is palak paneer good for diabetics?

Is paneer saagwala high in protein?
Can I eat paneer saagwala if I have high blood pressure?
What are the main ingredients in paneer saagwala?
How much sodium is in palak paneer?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN palak paneer data, which is the closest approved primary source for this Indian dish. Paneer saagwala and palak paneer are the same dish under different regional names; the adaptation is well-supported. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dish's macronutrient profile and paneer GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct measurement exists. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient values are adapted rather than an exact match from the source.
notes: 1. Paneer saagwala and palak paneer/saag paneer are the same dish; 'saagwala' means 'with greens/spinach' in Hindi. The canonical English name used here is Paneer Saagwala as per the input record, with saag paneer and palak paneer listed as primary aliases. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN palak paneer data; restaurant versions (such as the Bombay Grill listing on field survey data) may have higher fat and sodium due to additional cream and ghee. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~35, low) based on paneer GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and the low-carbohydrate, high-protein nature of the dish; no direct GI measurement for paneer saagwala was found in the approved GI database. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish. 6. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as paneer is a protein-rich dairy alternative used in the same dietary role as meat in Indian vegetarian cuisine. 7. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Indian cuisine served in Singapore context.
